coredata.dc:title: Rapid environmentally benign label free detection of heparin using highly fluorescent N,S-CDs sensing probe through a turn-on mechanism
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Abdella, Aya A
Zaki, Asmaa M
Hammad, Sherin
Mansour, Fotouh R.
coredata.dc:description: Heparin (HEP) is one of the oldest anticoagulant drugs that currently still in widespread clinical use. It lacks chromophore and not easily derivatized due to its hydrophilic nature. In this work we developed a green, selective, and sensitive fluorescence sensor for detection of HEP in its injection dosage forms. The sensor is composed of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CDs) semi quenched by Fe3+. The N,S-CDs were prepared using microwave assisted pyrolysis in 3.5 min and exhibited high emission at 425 nm after excitation at 350 nm with high quantum yield of 96%. Owing to the anionic nature of HEP, it could compete with N,S-CDs for Fe3+ complexation resulting in turning-on the quenched fluorescence. This fluorescence enhancement was linear over a concentration range between 6 and 20 μg/mL (R2 = 0.99) with a limit of detection of 1.41 µg/ml. The accuracy and precision of the proposed sensor were indicated by percentage recovery values between 98% −102% and %RSD less than 2, respectively. Furthermore, the proposed sensor was successfully applied for determination of HEP in injection dosage form. The developed sensor showed excellent greenness on analytical eco-scale (score 93%) and GAPI scale.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Co-doped carbon dot
Heparin
Fluorescence
Quenching
Turn-on sensor

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparation of N, S-CDs sensor solution
Preparation and characterization of N, S-CDs
Construction of N,S-CDs sensor for heparin detection
Optimization of HEP detection using the N,S-CDs-Fe3+ sensor
Application of the method dosage form
Green assessment of the proposed method

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Heparin chemical structure showing abundant sulfate and carboxylate groups which impart its metal complexation ability.

Overlay of excitation (solid red line), emission (solid blue line) and UV absorption (dashed line) spectra of the prepared N,S-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
FTIR spectrum showing surface function groups of the prepared N,S-CDs.

A) hrtem image of the prepared n,s-cds under 50 nm magnification power (inset; particle size distribution). b) SEAD pattern indicating the crystallinity of the prepared N,S-CDs. c) HRTEM image of N,S-CDs after Fe3+ addition (200 nm). d) TEM image of NS-CDs after Fe3+ addition (20 nm). e) HRTEM image of N,S-CDs, Fe3+/HEP (100 nm). f) HRTEM image of N,S-CDs-Fe3+/HEP (50 nm).

a) Effect of different metals on N,S-CDs fluorescence intensity. b) effect of added volume of Fe3+. c) Effect of N,S-CDs volume. d) HEP induced turn-on as a function of pH compared to distilled water.
Fluorescence enhancement in presence of different HEP sodium concentrations (6.00––20.00 μg/mL) on the N,S-CDs semi quenched by ferric chloride.
Accuracy and precision for HEP determination using the developed N,S-CDs-Fe3+ sensor.
Assay of HEP in Heparin sodium injection using the proposed N,S-CDs sensor.
Assessment the greenness of the developed method according to the analytical Eco-scale.

head.ce:title: Rapid environmentally benign label free detection of heparin using highly fluorescent N,S-CDs sensing probe through a turn-on mechanism

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, 31111, Egypt
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Pharmaceutical Analytical Chemistry, Faculty of Pharmacy, Tanta University, 31111, Egypt

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Heparin (HEP) is one of the oldest anticoagulant drugs that currently still in widespread clinical use. It lacks chromophore and not easily derivatized due to its hydrophilic nature. In this work we developed a green, selective, and sensitive fluorescence sensor for detection of HEP in its injection dosage forms. The sensor is composed of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CDs) semi quenched by Fe3+. The N,S-CDs were prepared using microwave assisted pyrolysis in 3.5 min and exhibited high emission at 425 nm after excitation at 350 nm with high quantum yield of 96%. Owing to the anionic nature of HEP, it could compete with N,S-CDs for Fe3+ complexation resulting in turning-on the quenched fluorescence. This fluorescence enhancement was linear over a concentration range between 6 and 20 μg/mL (R2 = 0.99) with a limit of detection of 1.41 µg/ml. The accuracy and precision of the proposed sensor were indicated by percentage recovery values between 98% −102% and %RSD less than 2, respectively. Furthermore, the proposed sensor was successfully applied for determination of HEP in injection dosage form. The developed sensor showed excellent greenness on analytical eco-scale (score 93%) and GAPI scale.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Heparin (HEP) is a linear, highly sulfated, anionic glycosaminoglycan with various pharmacological activities (Fig. 1 ). These pharmacological activities are due to heparin’s ability to interact with multiple proteins selectively. Interaction with these proteins is responsible for its anticoagulant and other activities [1]. Anticoagulant activity is due to heparin's ability to bind to antithrombin III (AT-III), which inhibits factor IIa (thrombin), factor Xa, and several other clotting factors [2]. Non-anticoagulant activities include anti-viral, anti-neoplastic and anti-inflammatory activities. It has been reported to decrease mortality in COVID-19 patients with sepsis-induced hypercoagulation [1]. These wide clinical applications make it of great interest in pharmaceutical analysis emphasizing the importance of the quality control of HEP.

HEP is the most anionic polysaccharide among all biomolecules, with average sulfation unit of 2.7 per disaccharide, rendering it highly water soluble [3]. It lacks chromophore and not easily derivatized due to its hydrophilic nature and complex structure. Few methods are reported in literature for its determination including, HPLC/resonance light scattering detection [4], strong anion exchange (SAX) chromatography [5], fluorometry [6,7], colorimetry [7,8], and electrochemical assay [9]. Although most of these methods are effective for the determination of HEP, but still have limitations including expensive instrumentation and technique, being time consuming, complex, not environmentally safe by using organic dyes, or encompassing special reagents so it is important to develop much simpler analytical method for HEP determination [10]. For these reasons, HEP can benefit from the newly introduced sensing probes that enabled the determination of numerous non-fluorescent compounds [11–15].

Carbon quantum dots (CDs), as a new class of quantum dots (QDs), are fluorescent, zero-dimensional carbon nanoparticles less than 10 nm in size [16]. CDs have recently attracted great attention due to their advantages like low or non-toxicity, high water solubility, ease of surface functionalization, optical and chemical stability, biocompatibility and environmental compatibility [16,17]. CDs have been widely applied in electrocatalysis [18,19] chemical sensors, biosensors and bioimaging[20]. Synthesis of CDs was performed using several methods. Microwave preparation method is reported to be more facile, cheap, simple and robust large scale synthesis of CDs [21]. Moreover, using microwave assisted methods enabled the fast and reproducible carbon dot preparation with high doping efficiency. Recently, the application of carbon dots-based sensors has enabled the sensitive detection of such challenging compounds. Nevertheless, some of the reported CDs-based sensors require too long heating (12 h) at elevated temperature (200 ℃) in special microwave reactor or hydrothermal autoclave [22,23]. In addition, other sensing platforms are incorporating environmentally hazardous dyes and semiconductor metals such as Cd and Te [24,25].

This work aimed to develop a green, environmentally benign, sensitive, rapid, cost-effective spectrofluorimetric method for determination of HEP employing nitrogen and sulfur doped carbon quantum dots (N,S-CDs). The rapid preparation of N,S-CDs was attempted employing a domestic microwave oven utilizing citric acid as carbon source and cysteine amino acid as nitrogen and sulfur source. Ferric ion was added to the fluorescent N,S-CDs resulting in marked fluorescence quenching, which was restored upon subsequent addition of HEP, due to its metal complexation ability. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first report of environmentally benign carbon dot-based sensor for determination of HEP.

HEP sodium (193 IU/mg) was obtained from AppliChem GmbH (Germany). L-cysteine (99%) was purchased from SRL (Mumbai, India), Citric acid (99%) was supplied by ISO-CHEM (Egypt). Ferric chloride (98% purity; SRL, Mumbai, India), magnesium sulfate and calcium chloride (99% purity; ISO-CHEM, Egypt), copper sulfate penta-hydrate (99%purity; El-Fara′ana, Egypt), zinc (98% purity; El-Fara′ana, Egypt), mercuric chloride (99% purity; Universal fine chemicals, Mumbai, India), nitric acid (55% assay; ISO-CHEM, Egypt), sodium hydroxide (99% purity; ISO-CHEM, Egypt), benzyl alcohol (99.8% purity; Riedel-de Haën, Germany), methyl paraben (99.98% purity; Pharmalog, China), propyl paraben (99.98% purity; Pharmalog, China), sodium chloride (99.5% purity; ISO-CHEM, Egypt), potassium chloride (99.5% purity; ISO-CHEM, Egypt) quinine sulfate (Alpha Chemica, Mumbai, India), acetic acid (99%purity, El-Fara′ana, Cairo, Egypt), sodium acetate (99% assay; ISO-CHEM, Egypt) were utilized. Heparin sodium injection (5000 IU, El-Nile Company, Cairo, Egypt) was purchased from local pharmacy. Dialysis membrane cutoff 12–14 KDa (FREY Scientific, US) was used.

A JASCO model FP-6300 spectrofluorometer (Tokyo, Japan), connected to a PC and equipped with xenon lamp, and operated with Spectra Manager software V1.53.01. The scanning parameters include an excitation and emission band width of 5 nm, a scanning speed of 1000 nm/min, at high sensitivity and medium response. The maximum excitation wavelength (λex) was 350 nm and maximum emission wavelength (λem) was (425) nm. UV absorption spectra were obtained using a JASCO V-530 double beam UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Tokyo, Japan). A JEOL High-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM) was used for N,S-CDs visualization and particle size measurement (Tokyo, Japan). A Jasco Fourier transform-infrared spectrum (FTIR-4100) was utilized to identify the surface functional groups of the N,S-CDs (Tokyo, Japan). A Tornado domestic microwave oven (1000 W) (Banha, Egypt), Branson-3510 ultrasonic cleaner (USA) and a Hettich benchtop centrifuge (Germany) were used during the N,S-CDs synthesis. Digital analytical balance (Sartorius AG, Germany) and Automatic Water Still (Sci Finetech, Seoul, South Korea) were used. HANNA pH 211 Microprocessor pH-meter with double junction glass electrode calibrated with standard buffers was used for adjusting the pH of the solutions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of N, S-CDs sensor solution
ce:section.ce:para: Synthesis of N,S-CDs was done using microwave-assisted approach according to a reported method [26] with some modifications as follow: 0.2 g citric acid were dissolved in 3 ml distilled water, then 0.1 g cysteine was added to the solution and Sonicated for 2 min for efficient mixing. Then, the solution was heated in domestic microwave at 500 W for 3.5 min till obtaining a yellowish foamy solid product. The produced solid was resuspended in 2 ml distilled water and centrifugated at 6000 rpm for 3 min then dialyzed against 50 ml distilled water for 2 h. The N,S-CDs solution was collected in dry clean glass bottle and was stored in refrigerator at 4◦c for further use.

The quantum yield (QY) of N,S-CDs was measured using quinine dissolved in 0.1 M H2SO4 as standard that are known for QY values as 54% at 350 nm excitation wavelength for quinine sulfate. The relative QY of N,S-CDs was calculated using Eq
(1)
[27,28].
(1)
Q X
=
Q
st
×
F x
F
st
×
η x
η
st
2
×
A
st
A x
where F refers to the fluorescence, A is the absorbance, x refers to N,S-CDs, st refers to quinine sulfate and η refers to the refractive index of the solvent.
The prepared N,S-CDs solution was diluted by quantitatively transferring 100 µl to 5 ml volumetric flask and making up the solution to total volume using distilled water.

General procedures for HEP detection: Different aliquots (30–100 μl) of HEP stock solution (1 mg/ml), 30 μl of ferric chloride solution (0.018 M) and 150 μl of diluted N,S-CDs solution were transferred to 5 ml flask, then the volume was completed to the final volume with the distilled water to obtain final HEP concentrations in the range of 6.00–20.00 μg/ml. The solution was vortex mixed and fluorescence intensities (F) were measured at 429 nm after excitation at 350 nm. A calibration curve was constructed by plotting F/F0 ratio vs the corresponding HEP concentration, where F0 and F are the florescence intensity of N,S-CDs-Fe3+ before and after addition of HEP, respectively.

For dosage form assay, Heparin sodium injection was diluted with distilled water to obtain a final concentration of 1 mg/ml. Different aliquots (40, 60, and 80 μl) of the diluted injection solution were analyzed following the above-mentioned procedures for HEP detection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of N, S-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: N,S-CDs preparation was performed using microwave assisted procedures. Compared to other bottom-up approaches, microwave can result in reduction in reaction time to the order of minutes rather than hours (2–12 h) reported with solvo/hydrothermal approach [29]. The reaction time was studied, and the optimum reaction time was 3.5 mins. Shorter heating time was not sufficient to achieve carbonization while longer time led to char formation. After their preparation, the N,S-CDs were purified by dialysis in order to get rid of the molecular organic fluorophores formed during the carbonization process [30]. Moreover, five N,S-CDs preparation cycles were performed and reproducible fluorescence intensity values were obtained.

The UV spectrum of N,S-CDs solution was recorded to characterize the prepared N,S-CDs. As shown in Fig. 2 , N,S-CDs have absorption peaks at 210 nm due to π-π* electron transition of C = C and 350 nm attributed to n-π* electron transition of C = O and C = S bonds [31,32]. The emission spectra of N,S-CDs were measured at different excitation wavelengths (320–370 nm) to study the effect of the excitation wavelength. The formed N,S-CDs exhibited excitation independent emission [33], as indicated in Fig. S1 , which is a characteristic property for CDs with very narrow particle size distribution [33]. The maximum emission was observed at an excitation wavelength of 350 nm. The QY of the formed N,S-CDs was found to be 96%. Such high QY values are reported for heteroatom doped CDs [34].

Identification of surface functional groups was accomplished using FTIR spectroscopy Fig. 3 . The stretch band from 3200 to 3500 cm−1 is characteristic for –OH and –NH stretching vibrations of the CDs. The peak at 1640 cm−1 can be attributed to C = O stretching vibrations. The band at 2493 cm−1 is attributed to the -SH group, while the band at 684 cm−1 could be due to the C-S stretching vibrations [26], indicating successful doping with sulfur.

Visualization, size distribution and particle size determination of the produced CDs were shown using HRTEM. As shown in Fig. 4
a, the prepared N,S-CDs were homogenous, spherical shaped and monodispersed with average particle size distribution around 3.83 nm ± 0.82. The crystallinity of the formed N,S-CDs was proven through SAED (Fig. 4 b).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Construction of N,S-CDs sensor for heparin detection
Optimization of HEP detection using the N,S-CDs-Fe3+ sensor
ce:section.ce:para: Different factors, including Fe3+ volume, N,S-CDs volume and pH, were studied to maximize the measured response and consequently sensor sensitivity. The added amount of Fe3+ could critically affect sensor sensitivity. Therefore, different volumes of Fe3+solution (10,20, 30, 40, 50 µl) were added. As shown in Fig. 5 b, 30 µl of Fe3+ was found optimum and achieved the maximum F/F0 ratio where semi-quenched N,S-CDs were obtained [37]. Larger Fe3+ volumes resulted in full quenching and subsequently reduced sensor sensitivity towards HEP. Moreover, the concentration of N,S-CDs was investigated. Different volumes of the diluted N,S-CDs solution (50, 100, 150, 200, 250, 300 µl) were tested. As shown in Fig. 5 c, maximum response was attained using 150 µl and 200 µl. The optimum CDs volume was decided as 150 µl to avoid the primary inner filter effect of N,S-CDs [38,39], which would probably occur at high concentrations of N,S-CDs and interfere with HEP analysis. Furthermore, the effect of pH was investigated by measuring the HEP induced fluorescence turn-on (F/F0) at different pH values. The effect of acetate buffer with different pH range was studied. Acetate buffer was excluded because minimum quenching activity was observed after acetate buffer addition Fig. S4 . This sudden decrease in Fe3+ quenching activity can be attributed to the formation of iron-carboxylate complex [40] which makes ferric ion not available in the reaction media. The pH of media was then adjusted using HNO3 and NaOH within pH range (2.5 to 6) to avoid the previous complex formation and fluorescence was detected and compared to distilled water effect Fig. S5 . Results obtained from distilled water were higher than those of other pH adjusted solutions as shown in Fig. 5 d, Fig. S4 and Fig. S5 . Therefore, non-buffered distilled water was utilized as dilution solvent for HEP sensing.

The proposed method was subsequently validated according to the International Council for Harmonization(ICH) Q2 (R1) guidelines for analytical method validation [41,42], including linearity, range, accuracy, precision, limit of detection (DL), limit of quantitation (QL), and selectivity.

In terms of linearity, the ability of HEP to turn-on the quenched N,S-CDs in a concentration dependent manner was observed over a concentration range between 6 and 20 μg/mL (Fig. 6 ). Regression parameters were calculated including slope, intercept, and residual standard deviation (Table 1 ). Good correlation was indicated by a correlation coefficient of 0.99 ( Table S1 and Fig. S6 ). Furthermore, the detection limit (DL) was calculated according to ICH Q2 guidelines using the formula (
D L = 3.3 ∗ σ / s
, where
σ
is SD of intercept and S is the slope of the calibration curve. The DL was found to be 1.41 µg/mL, indicating that the proposed method is sensitive towards HEP.

Additionally, the accuracy and precision of the developed sensor were evaluated by analysis of three different concentrations covering the full linearity range, each of three replicates. The mean % recovery was determined for each concentration level and was found within the range (98–102 %) which ensures the accuracy of the developed method (Table 1). The precision was indicated by %RSD less than 2 % (Table 1). The obtained results indicated the potential application of the developed method to determine HEP in its pharmaceutical dosage form, HEP sodium injection.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Application of the method dosage form
Green assessment of the proposed method
ce:section.ce:para: The greenness of the developed method was evaluated using the analytical eco-scale [43] and the green analytical procedure index (GAPI) [44]. The analytical eco-scale is a semi quantitative approach for green organic synthesis evaluation [45]. The basis for the concept of an analytical Eco-Scale is that the ideal green analysis has a value of 100. Penalty points are assigned for each of the valuated parameters, including amounts of reagents, hazards, energy and waste, that differs from “the ideal value”, lowering the total score Table 3 . The higher the score, the greener and more economical is the organic preparation [45]. The method greenness was also assessed by GAPI. In GAPI, a specific symbol with five pentagrams can be used to evaluate and quantify the environmental impact involved in each step of an analytical methodology, mainly from green through yellow to red indicating low, medium to high impact, respectively [44]. The developed method eco-scale score was 93% (Table 3) indicating excellent method greenness. Assessment of the method using GAPI showed that the method was green since the solvent used in this study was distilled water and the method did not involve any extraction, sample preparation or preservation, high energy instrumentation, or occupational hazard, with less than 10 ml waste per sample ( Fig. S8 ).

In this study, an environmentally benign reliable sensitive and selective spectrofluorimetric probe was developed for detection of HEP. The sensing probe is mainly based on the highly fluorescent N and S doped CDs coupled to Fe3+. The addition of HEP to N,S-CDs-Fe3+ restored the fluorescence emission as a result of HEP-Fe3+ complex formation. Factors affecting the sensor ability to detect HEP were studied and optimized. The developed sensor has been successfully applied for the quantitation of HEP in pure form and pharmaceutical dosage form with acceptable accuracy and precision. The developed sensor showed excellent greenness on both analytical Eco-scale and GAPI. The developed method is green, simple, rapid, and cost effective that can be easily applied for routine analysis of HEP.
This research did not receive any specific grant from funding agencies in the public, commercial, or not-for-profit sectors.

Aya A Abdella: Methodology, Investigation, Validation. Asmaa M Zaki: Formal analysis, Data curation, Methodology, Investigation, Validation, Writing – original draft. Sherin Hammad: Resources, Project administration. Fotouh R. Mansour: Conceptualization, Project administration.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123609.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
